UNLOCKING DOORS WEB DESIGN

Rebuilding lives.

Product Design Prototyping Mockups User Research Front-end

TIMELINE

Aug 2023 - Dec 2023

ROLE

UI/UX Designer, Front-end Developer

PLATFORM

Website

⚪ Problem

Non-profit organization, Unlocking Doors, has too much data in too many places.
Unlocking Doors is an organization that provides its clients, whom are ex-convicts, connections and resources to help them get back on their feet. They came to the Engineering Projects in Community Service program at UT Dallas with the problem that all of their resources are scattered throughout hundreds of different Excel sheets - making it extremely difficult for them to efficiently retrieve contacts for their clients.

⚪ Solution

The new Unlocking Doors database will streamline all of the company's contacts into one place, improving all of its users' search experience.

This project was previously worked on by a different team - however, along with requesting other features, the organization's workers were not satisfied with its design.

⚪ Research

Pt. 1 - Assessing the issue via interview
To further understand the organization's requests, I met with two of the database's users to discuss what needs to be improved. They explained that they work on this application all day, but they found that the previous team's interface was dull and boring - they wanted a modernized interface that was easier on their eyes.

Interview Questions
  1. What do you want to accomplish with the redesigned database?
  2. Which specific tasks do you perform most frequently within the database?
  3. Can you describe any pain points or frustrations you have with the previous design?
  4. What is your vision for the overall look and feel of the database?
  5. Are there any design elements or styles (e.g., colors, layouts) that you prefer or want to avoid?
  6. Can you explain how you would like the navigation or user flow to improve?
  7. Are there any specific features that didn’t meet expectations in the previous version?
  8. You mentioned the previous design felt dull—can you describe what a "modernized" interface means to you?
  9. How long do you typically spend using the database, and what would make it more efficient or less tedious during extended use?

One of our current team members gave me insight to the cause of the issue - the last team did not have anyone that specialized in UI/UX. I took a look at the previous semester's prototypes and analyzed what could have been improved.
Upon reviewing and after conducting the interviews, I found that the prototypes lacked two main characteristics that modern interfaces typically have: rounded corners and high color contrast. Additionally, since the database is going to house a lot of information, I wanted to make sure I organized all of the site's contacts and features in an intuitive and readable manner for the employees.
Findings:

Users find that the previous design was dull, lacked visual appeal, and wasn’t user-friendly for those working in the application daily. Employees requested a modernized interface with rounded corners and higher color contrast to reduce eye strain and make navigating the large volume of data more intuitive. Additionally, they wanted the design to be consistent with the organization's current branding, which includes the Poppins font and a royal blue color scheme, as seen on their website. I created a mood board to help me visualize all the users' needs.

⚪ Design
User Flow
A user flow already existed for the site that was pre-approved by its users (the employees), so no changes were made to it:

Wireframing
Since the organization did not want to make any new changes in the user flow, I began the design process with redesigning some of the wireframes:

Mockup
Using the wireframes, I began making changes to the previous team's prototypes. I opted for a darker blue + white + black color palette to achieve a more modern look, and, as requested, went back to round corners where necessary:

⚪ User Testing

I took these prototypes to the Unlocking Doors corporate office and presented them to Christina and two other employees that will be using the database. The overall takeaway was that they were all very satisfied with the interface and surprisingly had no complaints. However, I kept in touch with them throughout the rest of the timeline to get updates on any potential changes they wanted later on. I also presented the prototypes to the rest of the development team to ensure that they could be implemented within the given timeline.

⚪ Implementation

Despite the success of the wireframes and prototypes with our client, implementation turned out to be buggy. I also worked as a front-end dev for the project along with two others, and the interface was fully built early on in the duration of the project - however, features often changed from the back-end of things, and it was challenging to keep up with these changes before our deadline. Because of this, the project will prolong for another semester with a different team of developers (who will continue to use these designs).

⚪ Working Demo

The implementation of this application was completed by the next semester of developers and is not available for public view. However, you can read more about the project here!
